revision.text: #redirect[[en:Ōkuchi_no_Magami]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''真神'''（まかみ）は、現在は絶滅してしまった日本[[狼]]が神格化したもの。'''大口真神'''（おおぐちまがみ）。
真神は古来より[[聖獣]]として崇拝され、大和国（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある飛鳥の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化され、[[猪]]や[[鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護する神とされた。
人語を理解し、人間の性質を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、悪人を罰するものと信仰された。しかし時代が流れ、人間が山地まで生活圏を広げると、狼は人と家畜を襲うものだという認識が広まった。いつしか女性に言い寄り悪事を企む者を「送り狼」と呼ぶようになった時、真神の神聖さは地に落ちていった。
[[Category:日本の神|まかみ]]

'''真神'''（まかみ）は、現在は絶滅してしまった日本[[狼]]が神格化したもの。'''大口真神'''（おおぐちまがみ）。
真神は古来より[[聖獣]]として崇拝され、大和国（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある飛鳥の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化され、[[猪]]や[[鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護するものとされた。
人語を理解し、人間の性質を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、悪人を罰するものと信仰された。また、厄除け、特に火難や盗難から守る力が強いとされ、[[絵馬]]などにも描かれてきた。しかし時代が流れ、人間が山地まで生活圏を広げると、狼は人と家畜を襲うものだという認識が広まった。いつしか女性に言い寄り悪事を企む者を「送り狼」と呼ぶようになった時、真神の神聖さは地に落ちていった。
[[Category:日本の神|まかみ]]

'''真神'''（まかみ）は、現在は絶滅してしまった日本[[狼]]が神格化したもの。'''大口真神'''（おおぐちまがみ）。
真神は古来より[[聖獣]]として崇拝され、大和国（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある飛鳥の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化され、[[猪]]や[[鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護するものとされた。
人語を理解し、人間の性質を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、悪人を罰するものと信仰された。また、厄除け、特に火難や盗難から守る力が強いとされ、[[絵馬]]などにも描かれてきた。しかし時代が流れ、人間が山地まで生活圏を広げると、狼は人と家畜を襲うものだという認識が広まった。そして狼の数が減っていくにしたがって、真神の神聖さは地に落ちていった。
[[Category:日本の神|まかみ]]

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|日本狼が神格化したもの|岡山県美作市にある地名|福本村}} '''真神'''（まかみ）は、現在は絶滅してしまった[[ニホンオオカミ|日本狼]]が[[神格化]]したもの。'''大口真神'''（おおぐちまがみ）とも呼ばれる。
真神は古来より聖獣として崇拝された。大和国（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある飛鳥の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化され、[[イノシシ|猪]]や[[シカ|鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護するものとされた。
人語を理解し、人間の性質を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、悪人を罰するものと信仰された。また、厄除け、特に火難や盗難から守る力が強いとされ、[[絵馬]]などにも描かれてきた。しかし時代が流れ、人間が山地まで生活圏を広げると、狼は人と家畜を襲うものだという認識が広まった。そして狼の数が減っていくにしたがって、真神の神聖さは地に落ちていったという。
==関連項目== *[[三峯神社]] （埼玉県秩父郡大滝村三峰）大口真神を祀る *[[山住神社]] （静岡県磐田郡水窪町山住）大口真神を祀る *[[大川神社]] （京都府舞鶴市大川） 大口真神を祀る *[[釜山神社]] （埼玉県大里郡寄居町）大口真神を祀る
==外部リンク== *ETV特集「見狼記」 http://www.nhk.or.jp/etv21c/file/2012/0219.html
{{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まかみ}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:神話・伝説の狼]] [[Category:厄除け]]
[[ru:Оками (мифология)]] [[uk:Окамі]] [[zh:真神]]

{{Otheruses|日本狼が神格化したもの|岡山県美作市にある地名|福本村}} '''真神'''（まかみ）は、現在は絶滅してしまった[[ニホンオオカミ|日本狼]]が[[神格化]]したもの。'''大口真神'''（おおぐちまかみ）とも呼ばれる。
真神は古来より聖獣として[[崇拝]]された。[[大和国]]（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある[[飛鳥|飛鳥]]の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化され、[[イノシシ|猪]]や[[シカ|鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護するものとされた。
人語を理解し、[[人間]]の[[性質]]を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、[[悪人]]を[[罰]]するものと信仰された。また、[[厄除け]]、特に[[火災|火難]]や[[窃盗|盗難]]から守る力が強いとされ、[[絵馬]]などにも描かれてきた。しかし[[時代]]が流れ、人間が[[山地]]まで[[生活圏]]を広げると、狼は人と[[家畜]]を襲うものだという認識が広まった。そして狼の数が減っていくにしたがって、真神の神聖さは地に落ちていったという。

{{Otheruses|日本狼が神格化したもの|岡山県美作市にある地名|福本村}} '''真神'''（まかみ）は、日本に生息していた[[ニホンオオカミ|狼]]（ニホンオオカミ）が[[神格化]]したもの。'''大口真神'''（おおくちのまがみ、おおぐちまかみ）、'''御神犬'''とも呼ばれる。
真神は古来より聖獣として[[崇拝]]された。[[大和国]]（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある[[飛鳥|飛鳥]]の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化され、[[イノシシ|猪]]や[[シカ|鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護するものとされた。
人語を理解し、[[人間]]の[[性質]]を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、[[悪人]]を[[罰]]するものと信仰された。また、[[厄除け]]、特に[[火災|火難]]や[[窃盗|盗難]]から守る力が強いとされ、[[絵馬]]などにも描かれてきた。
現在も[[埼玉県]][[秩父地方]]の神社を中心に、狼が描かれた[[神札]]（お札）が頒布され、信仰を集めている<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html お札になったオオカミ]</ref>。

{{Otheruses|日本狼が神格化したもの|岡山県美作市にある地名|福本村}} '''真神'''（まかみ）は、日本に生息していた[[ニホンオオカミ|狼]]（ニホンオオカミ）が[[神格化]]したもの。'''大口真神'''（おおくちのまがみ、おおぐちまかみ）、'''御神犬'''とも呼ばれる。
真神は古来より聖獣として[[崇拝]]された。[[大和国]]（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある[[飛鳥|飛鳥]]の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化され<ref>大和国[[風土記]]の[[逸文]]（『[[枕詞燭明抄]]（中）』）に記述され、「大口の真神原」という地名説話として語られ、[[飛鳥大仏]]辺りの古称で『[[万葉集]]』にも見られる。『風土記 [[日本古典文学大系]]2』 [[岩波書店]] 14刷1971年（1刷1958年） p.421の脚注。</ref>、[[イノシシ|猪]]や[[シカ|鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護するものとされた。
人語を理解し、[[人間]]の[[性質]]を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、[[悪人]]を[[罰]]するものと信仰された。また、[[厄除け]]、特に[[火災|火難]]や[[窃盗|盗難]]から守る力が強いとされ、[[絵馬]]などにも描かれてきた。
現在も[[埼玉県]][[秩父地方]]の神社を中心に、狼が描かれた[[神札]]（お札）が頒布され、信仰を集めている<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html お札になったオオカミ]</ref>。
== 脚注 == <references />

== 外部リンク == * [[ETV特集]]「見狼記」 http://www.nhk.or.jp/etv21c/file/2012/0219.html

真神は古来より聖獣として[[崇拝]]された。[[大和国]]（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある[[飛鳥|飛鳥]]の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化され<ref>[[大和国風土記]]の[[逸文]]（『[[枕詞燭明抄]]（中）』）に記述され、「大口の真神原」という地名説話として語られ、[[飛鳥大仏]]辺りの古称で『[[万葉集]]』にも見られる。『風土記 [[日本古典文学大系]]2』 [[岩波書店]] 14刷1971年（1刷1958年） p.421の脚注。</ref>、[[イノシシ|猪]]や[[シカ|鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護するものとされた。
人語を理解し、[[人間]]の[[性質]]を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、[[悪人]]を[[罰]]するものと信仰された。また、[[厄除け]]、特に[[火災|火難]]や[[窃盗|盗難]]から守る力が強いとされ、[[絵馬]]などにも描かれてきた。

{{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まかみ}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:神話・伝説の狼]] [[Category:厄除け]]

{{Otheruses|日本狼が神格化したもの|岡山県美作市にある地名|福本村}} '''真神'''（まかみ）は、日本に生息していた[[ニホンオオカミ|狼]]（ニホンオオカミ）が[[神格化]]したもの。'''大口真神'''（おおくちのまがみ、おおぐちまかみ）、'''御神犬'''とも呼ばれる。
真神は古来より聖獣として[[崇拝]]された。[[大和国]]（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある[[飛鳥|飛鳥]]の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化され<ref>[[大和国風土記]]の[[逸文]]（『[[枕詞燭明抄]]（中）』）に記述され、「大口の真神原」という地名説話として語られ、[[飛鳥大仏]]辺りの古称で『[[万葉集]]』にも見られる。『風土記 [[日本古典文学大系]]2』 [[岩波書店]] 14刷1971年（1刷1958年） p.421の脚注。</ref>、[[イノシシ|猪]]や[[シカ|鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護するものとされた。
『[[万葉集]]』巻八には「大口の まかみの原に ふる雪は いたくなふりそ 家もあらなくに」（[[舎人娘子]]）と記され、少なくとも（大和国風土記の逸文と合わせ）8世紀からみられる。
人語を理解し、[[人間]]の[[性質]]を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、[[悪人]]を[[罰]]するものと信仰された。また、[[厄除け]]、特に[[火災|火難]]や[[窃盗|盗難]]から守る力が強いとされ、[[絵馬]]などにも描かれてきた。
現在も[[埼玉県]][[秩父地方]]の神社を中心に、狼が描かれた[[神札]]（お札）が頒布され、信仰を集めている<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html お札になったオオカミ]</ref>。

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|日本狼が神格化したもの|岡山県美作市にある地名|福本村}} '''真神'''（まかみ）は、日本に生息していた[[ニホンオオカミ|狼]]（ニホンオオカミ）が[[神格化]]したもの。'''大口真神'''（おおくちのまがみ、おおぐちまかみ）、'''御神犬'''とも呼ばれる。
真神は古来より聖獣として[[崇拝]]された。[[大和国]]（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある[[飛鳥]]の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化され<ref>[[大和国風土記]]の[[逸文]]（『[[枕詞燭明抄]]（中）』）に記述され、「大口の真神原」という地名説話として語られ、[[飛鳥大仏]]辺りの古称で『[[万葉集]]』にも見られる。『風土記 [[日本古典文学大系]]2』 [[岩波書店]] 14刷1971年（1刷1958年） p.421の脚注。</ref>、[[イノシシ|猪]]や[[シカ|鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護するものとされた。
『[[万葉集]]』巻八には「大口の まかみの原に ふる雪は いたくなふりそ 家もあらなくに」（[[舎人娘子]]）と記され、少なくとも（大和国風土記の逸文と合わせ）8世紀からみられる。
人語を理解し、[[人間]]の[[性質]]を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、[[悪人]]を[[罰]]するものと信仰された。また、[[厄除け]]、特に[[火災|火難]]や[[窃盗|盗難]]から守る力が強いとされ、[[絵馬]]などにも描かれてきた。
現在も[[埼玉県]][[秩父地方]]の神社を中心に、狼が描かれた[[神札]]（お札）が頒布され、信仰を集めている<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html お札になったオオカミ]</ref>。

revision.comment: 副詞「古来」に後続する文末を、「崇拝された」から「崇拝されてきた」に改めた。
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|日本狼が神格化したもの|岡山県美作市にある地名|福本村}} '''真神'''（まかみ）は、日本に生息していた[[ニホンオオカミ|狼]]（ニホンオオカミ）が[[神格化]]したもの。'''大口真神'''（おおくちのまがみ、おおぐちまかみ）、'''御神犬'''とも呼ばれる。
真神は古来、聖獣として[[崇拝]]されてきた。[[大和国]]（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある[[飛鳥]]の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化され<ref>[[大和国風土記]]の[[逸文]]（『[[枕詞燭明抄]]（中）』）に記述され、「大口の真神原」という地名説話として語られ、[[飛鳥大仏]]辺りの古称で『[[万葉集]]』にも見られる。『風土記 [[日本古典文学大系]]2』 [[岩波書店]] 14刷1971年（1刷1958年） p.421の脚注。</ref>、[[イノシシ|猪]]や[[シカ|鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護するものとされた。
『[[万葉集]]』巻八には「大口の まかみの原に ふる雪は いたくなふりそ 家もあらなくに」（[[舎人娘子]]）と記され、少なくとも（大和国風土記の逸文と合わせ）8世紀からみられる。
人語を理解し、[[人間]]の[[性質]]を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、[[悪人]]を[[罰]]するものと信仰された。また、[[厄除け]]、特に[[火災|火難]]や[[窃盗|盗難]]から守る力が強いとされ、[[絵馬]]などにも描かれてきた。
現在も[[埼玉県]][[秩父地方]]の神社を中心に、狼が描かれた[[神札]]（お札）が頒布され、信仰を集めている<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html お札になったオオカミ]</ref>。

{{Otheruses|日本狼が神格化したもの|岡山県美作市にある地名|福本村}} '''真神'''（まかみ）は、日本に生息していた[[ニホンオオカミ|狼]]（ニホンオオカミ）が[[神格化]]したもの。'''大口真神'''（おおくちのまがみ、おおぐちまかみ）、'''御神犬'''とも呼ばれる。
真神は古来、聖獣として[[崇拝]]されてきた。[[大和国]]（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある[[飛鳥]]の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化され<ref>[[大和国風土記]]の[[逸文]]（『[[枕詞燭明抄]]（中）』）に記述され、「大口の真神原」という地名説話として語られ、[[飛鳥大仏]]辺りの古称で『[[万葉集]]』にも見られる。『風土記 [[日本古典文学大系]]2』 [[岩波書店]] 14刷1971年（1刷1958年） p.421の脚注。</ref>、[[イノシシ|猪]]や[[シカ|鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護するものとされた。
『[[万葉集]]』巻八には「大口の まかみの原に ふる雪は いたくなふりそ 家もあらなくに」（[[舎人娘子]]）と記され、少なくとも（大和国風土記の逸文と合わせ）8世紀からみられる。
人語を理解し、[[人間]]の[[性質]]を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、[[悪人]]を[[罰]]するものと信仰された。また、[[厄除け]]、特に[[火災|火難]]や[[窃盗|盗難]]から守る力が強いとされ、[[絵馬]]などにも描かれてきた。
現在も[[埼玉県]][[秩父地方]]の神社を中心に、狼が描かれた[[神札]]（お札）が頒布され、信仰を集めている<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html お札になったオオカミ]</ref>。このすぐ南の[[御岳山 (東京都)|武蔵御岳山]]上の[[武蔵御嶽神社]]には『日本書紀』の記述に基づく「おいぬ様」（または「お犬さま」）の伝説があり、[[ヤマトタケル#東征|日本武尊]]の東征の折、邪神が大鹿の姿で現れたのを[[ノビル|野蒜]]で退治したが、その時に大山鳴動して霧に巻かれて道に迷ったのを、そこに忽然と白い[[狼]]が表れて道案内をして、無事に日本武尊軍を無事に導いたので、尊は「大口真神としてそこに留まるように。」といったという。大口真神は[[江戸時代]]の[[天保]]年間ころより盗難除け・魔物除けとしての信仰が盛んになり、本殿のおいぬ様の[[狛犬]]はブロンズ製（1807年の作）で、本殿前の像は[[北村西望]]の作（1985年奉納）である。<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ 山に棲むオオカミへ、畏敬の念（武蔵御嶽神社）]</ref>
== 脚注 == <references />

== 外部リンク == * [[ETV特集]]「見狼記」 http://www.nhk.or.jp/etv21c/file/2012/0219.html

{{Otheruses|日本狼が神格化したもの|岡山県美作市にある地名|福本村}} '''真神'''（まかみ）は、日本に生息していた[[ニホンオオカミ|狼]]（ニホンオオカミ）が[[神格化]]したもの。'''大口真神'''（おおくちのまがみ、おおぐちまかみ）、'''御神犬'''とも呼ばれる。
真神は古来、聖獣として[[崇拝]]されてきた。また、[[イノシシ|猪]]や[[シカ|鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護するものとされた。人語を理解し、[[人間]]の[[性質]]を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、[[悪人]]を[[罰]]するものと信仰された。また、[[厄除け]]、特に[[火災|火難]]や[[窃盗|盗難]]から守る力が強いとされ、[[絵馬]]などにも描かれてきた。
[[大和国]]（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある[[飛鳥]]の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化された<ref>[[大和国風土記]]の[[逸文]]（『[[枕詞燭明抄]]（中）』）に記述され、「大口の真神原」という地名説話として語られ、[[飛鳥大仏]]辺りの古称で『[[万葉集]]』にも見られる。『風土記 [[日本古典文学大系]]2』 [[岩波書店]] 14刷1971年（1刷1958年） p.421の脚注。</ref>。『[[万葉集]]』巻八には「大口の まかみの原に ふる雪は いたくなふりそ 家もあらなくに」（[[舎人娘子]]）と記され、少なくとも（大和国風土記の逸文と合わせ）8世紀からみられる。この「大口の真神原」には後に飛鳥衣縫造祖樹葉（あすかきぬぬいのみやつこ の おや このは）の邸宅があったとされ、[[用明天皇]]の代に[[蘇我馬子]]が寺の建立を発願、[[崇峻天皇]]の代に館を廃し[[法興寺]]（後の[[飛鳥寺]]）の造営が始まったとされる。
現在も[[埼玉県]][[秩父地方]]の神社を中心に、狼が描かれた[[神札]]（お札）が頒布され、信仰を集めている<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html お札になったオオカミ]</ref>。このすぐ南の[[御岳山 (東京都)|武蔵御岳山]]上の[[武蔵御嶽神社]]には『日本書紀』の記述に基づく「おいぬ様」（または「お犬さま」）の伝説があり、[[ヤマトタケル#東征|日本武尊]]の東征の折、邪神が大鹿の姿で現れたのを[[ノビル|野蒜]]で退治したが、その時に大山鳴動して霧に巻かれて道に迷ったのを、そこに忽然と白い[[狼]]が表れて道案内をして、無事に日本武尊軍を無事に導いたので、尊は「大口真神としてそこに留まるように。」といったという。大口真神は[[江戸時代]]の[[天保]]年間ころより盗難除け・魔物除けとしての信仰が盛んになり、本殿のおいぬ様の[[狛犬]]はブロンズ製（1807年の作）で、本殿前の像は[[北村西望]]の作（1985年奉納）である。<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ 山に棲むオオカミへ、畏敬の念（武蔵御嶽神社）]</ref>

revision.comment: 「飛鳥衣縫造祖樹葉」をリンク。
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|日本狼が神格化したもの|岡山県美作市にある地名|福本村}} '''真神'''（まかみ）は、日本に生息していた[[ニホンオオカミ|狼]]（ニホンオオカミ）が[[神格化]]したもの。'''大口真神'''（おおくちのまがみ、おおぐちまかみ）、'''御神犬'''とも呼ばれる。
真神は古来、聖獣として[[崇拝]]されてきた。また、[[イノシシ|猪]]や[[シカ|鹿]]から[[作物]]を守護するものとされた。人語を理解し、[[人間]]の[[性質]]を見分ける力を有し、善人を守護し、[[悪人]]を[[罰]]するものと信仰された。また、[[厄除け]]、特に[[火災|火難]]や[[窃盗|盗難]]から守る力が強いとされ、[[絵馬]]などにも描かれてきた。
[[大和国]]（現在の[[奈良県]]）にある[[飛鳥]]の真神原の老狼は、大勢の人間を食べてきたため、その獰猛さから神格化された<ref>[[大和国風土記]]の[[逸文]]（『[[枕詞燭明抄]]（中）』）に記述され、「大口の真神原」という地名説話として語られ、[[飛鳥大仏]]辺りの古称で『[[万葉集]]』にも見られる。『風土記 [[日本古典文学大系]]2』 [[岩波書店]] 14刷1971年（1刷1958年） p.421の脚注。</ref>。『[[万葉集]]』巻八には「大口の まかみの原に ふる雪は いたくなふりそ 家もあらなくに」（[[舎人娘子]]）と記され、少なくとも（大和国風土記の逸文と合わせ）8世紀からみられる。この「大口の真神原」には後に[[飛鳥衣縫樹葉|飛鳥衣縫造祖樹葉]]（あすかきぬぬいのみやつこ の おや このは）の邸宅があったとされ、[[用明天皇]]の代に[[蘇我馬子]]が寺の建立を発願、[[崇峻天皇]]の代に館を廃し[[法興寺]]（後の[[飛鳥寺]]）の造営が始まったとされる。
現在も[[埼玉県]][[秩父地方]]の神社を中心に、狼が描かれた[[神札]]（お札）が頒布され、信仰を集めている<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html お札になったオオカミ]</ref>。このすぐ南の[[御岳山 (東京都)|武蔵御岳山]]上の[[武蔵御嶽神社]]には『日本書紀』の記述に基づく「おいぬ様」（または「お犬さま」）の伝説があり、[[ヤマトタケル#東征|日本武尊]]の東征の折、邪神が大鹿の姿で現れたのを[[ノビル|野蒜]]で退治したが、その時に大山鳴動して霧に巻かれて道に迷ったのを、そこに忽然と白い[[狼]]が表れて道案内をして、無事に日本武尊軍を無事に導いたので、尊は「大口真神としてそこに留まるように。」といったという。大口真神は[[江戸時代]]の[[天保]]年間ころより盗難除け・魔物除けとしての信仰が盛んになり、本殿のおいぬ様の[[狛犬]]はブロンズ製（1807年の作）で、本殿前の像は[[北村西望]]の作（1985年奉納）である。<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ 山に棲むオオカミへ、畏敬の念（武蔵御嶽神社）]</ref>
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[三峯神社]] - （埼玉県秩父郡大滝村三峰）大口真神を祀る * [[山住神社]] - （静岡県磐田郡水窪町山住）大口真神を祀る * [[大川神社]] - （京都府舞鶴市大川） 大口真神を祀る * [[釜山神社]] - （埼玉県大里郡寄居町）大口真神を祀る * [[武蔵御嶽神社]] - （東京都青梅市御岳山）大口真神を祀る
== 外部リンク == * [[ETV特集]]「見狼記」 http://www.nhk.or.jp/etv21c/file/2012/0219.html

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |lang=ja |author=Shogakukan |year=2020 |publisher=Shogakukan Kotobank |title=Revised Japanese Dictionary |chapter=Makami |ref=harv }} {{kotobank|1=Makami|2=Revised Japanese Dictionary}}
* {{Cite book |lang=ja |last=Matsumura |first=Akira |year=2020 |publisher=Shogakukan Kotobank |title=Digital Daijisen |chapter=Makami |ref=harv }} {{kotobank|1=Makami|2=Digital Daijisen}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Chichibu, Saitama Prefecture) * {{ill|Yamazumi Shrine (Hamamatsu)|ja|山住神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Tenryū Ward, Hamamatsu, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Okawa Shrine (Maizuru)|ja|大川神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Maizuru, Kyoto Prefecture) * {{ill|Kamayama Shrine|ja|釜山神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Yorii, Saitama Prefecture) * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Mount Mitake, Tokyo)
== External Links == * [[ETV Special]]: "Wolf Chronicles" [https://www.nhk.or.jp/etv21c/file/2012/0219.html]

{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |lang=ja |author=Shogakukan |year=2020 |publisher=Shogakukan Kotobank |title=Revised Japanese Dictionary |chapter=Makami |ref=harv }} {{kotobank|1=Makami|2=Revised Japanese Dictionary}}
* {{Cite book |lang=ja |last=Matsumura |first=Akira |year=2020 |publisher=Shogakukan Kotobank |title=Digital Daijisen |chapter=Makami |ref=harv }} {{kotobank|1=Makami|2=Digital Daijisen}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Chichibu, Saitama Prefecture) * {{ill|Yamazumi Shrine (Hamamatsu)|ja|山住神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Tenryū Ward, Hamamatsu, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Okawa Shrine (Maizuru)|ja|大川神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Maizuru, Kyoto Prefecture) * {{ill|Kamayama Shrine|ja|釜山神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Yorii, Saitama Prefecture) * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Mount Mitake, Tokyo)
== External Links == * [[ETV Special]]: "Wolf Chronicles" [https://www.nhk.or.jp/etv21c/file/2012/0219.html]

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}

* {{Cite book |lang=ja |last=Matsumura |first=Akira |year=2020 |publisher=Shogakukan Kotobank |title=Digital Daijisen |chapter=Makami |ref=harv }} {{kotobank|1=Makami|2=Digital Daijisen}}

{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}

* {{Cite book |lang=ja |last=Matsumura |first=Akira |year=2020 |publisher=Shogakukan Kotobank |title=Digital Daijisen |chapter=Makami |ref=harv }} {{kotobank|1=Makami|2=Digital Daijisen}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Chichibu, Saitama Prefecture) * {{ill|Yamazumi Shrine (Hamamatsu)|ja|山住神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Tenryū Ward, Hamamatsu, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Okawa Shrine (Maizuru)|ja|大川神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Maizuru, Kyoto Prefecture) * {{ill|Kamayama Shrine|ja|釜山神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Yorii, Saitama Prefecture) * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Mount Mitake, Tokyo)

{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |lang=ja |author=Shogakukan |year=2020 |publisher=Shogakukan Kotobank |title=Revised Japanese Dictionary |chapter=Makami |ref=harv }} {{kotobank|1=Makami|2=Revised Japanese Dictionary}}
* {{Cite book |lang=ja |last=Matsumura |first=Akira |year=2020 |publisher=Shogakukan Kotobank |title=Digital Daijisen |chapter=Makami |ref=harv }} {{kotobank|1=Makami|2=Digital Daijisen}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Chichibu, Saitama Prefecture) * {{ill|Yamazumi Shrine (Hamamatsu)|ja|山住神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Tenryū Ward, Hamamatsu, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Okawa Shrine (Maizuru)|ja|大川神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Maizuru, Kyoto Prefecture) * {{ill|Kamayama Shrine|ja|釜山神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Yorii, Saitama Prefecture) * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Mount Mitake, Tokyo)
== External Links == * [[ETV Special]]: "Wolf Chronicles" [https://www.nhk.or.jp/etv21c/file/2012/0219.html]

{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.
== References ==
{{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |lang=ja |author=Shogakukan |year=2020 |publisher=Shogakukan Kotobank |title=Revised Japanese Dictionary |chapter=Makami |ref=harv }} {{kotobank|1=Makami|2=Revised Japanese Dictionary}}
* {{Cite book |lang=ja |last=Matsumura |first=Akira |year=2020 |publisher=Shogakukan Kotobank |title=Digital Daijisen |chapter=Makami |ref=harv }} {{kotobank|1=Makami|2=Digital Daijisen}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Chichibu, Saitama Prefecture) * {{ill|Yamazumi Shrine (Hamamatsu)|ja|山住神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Tenryū Ward, Hamamatsu, Shizuoka Prefecture) * {{ill|Okawa Shrine (Maizuru)|ja|大川神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Maizuru, Kyoto Prefecture) * {{ill|Kamayama Shrine|ja|釜山神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Yorii, Saitama Prefecture) * {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} – Dedicated to Ōkuchi no Makami (Mount Mitake, Tokyo)
== External Links == * [[ETV Special]]: "Wolf Chronicles" [https://www.nhk.or.jp/etv21c/file/2012/0219.html]

It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".

It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.

{{Otheruses|Wolf deified in Japanese mythology|A place name in Mimasaka City, Okayama Prefecture|Fukumoto Village}} '''Makami''' (真神{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, まがみ{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}, しんじん{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}) refers to the [[Japanese wolf|wolf]] (ニホンオオカミ) as its [[ancient name]] or [[alternative name]]{{sfn|Matsumura|2020|p=Makami}}{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}, meaning "true [[god]]" or "righteous god"{{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}. It is also known as "'''Ōkuchi no Makami''' (大口真神)" and "'''Sacred Dog''' (御神犬)".
== Overview ==
Makami refers to the deification of the wolves (Japanese wolves) that once lived in Japan. Since ancient times, Makami has been worshiped as a [[sacred beast]]. It was considered a protector of [[crops]] against [[wild boars|boars]] and [[deer]], capable of understanding human speech, discerning human [[nature]], guarding good people, and punishing [[evil]] ones. Makami was also believed to provide [[protection]] against [[misfortune]], particularly [[fire|fires]] and [[theft]], and was often depicted on [[ema]] (votive plaques){{sfn|Shogakukan|2020|p=Makami}}.
In [[Yamato Province]] (modern [[Nara Prefecture]]), the "Makami no Hara" (Makami Field) in [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]] was home to an old wolf that devoured many people, leading to its deification because of its ferocity<ref>{{ill|Yamato Fudoki|ja|大和国風土記}} fragment (quoted in 『{{ill|Makurakotoba Shokumeishō|ja|枕詞燭明抄}}』), which describes a place name legend "Ōkuchi no Makami no Hara." 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』 also refers to this area, indicating its recognition from the 8th century onward.</ref>. In 『{{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}』, Volume 8, it is written: > "Ōkuchi no / Makami no Hara ni / Furu yuki wa / Itaku na furiso / Ie mo aranaku ni" > ("Snow falls deeply / Upon Ōkuchi Makami Field, / Yet do not let it fall so heavily, / For there are no homes here.") – [[Toneriko Daughter|舎人娘子]]
This area, later associated with the estate of [[Asuka Kinenoi|Asuka Kinunui no Mikoto no Oya Konoha]], became the site where [[Prince Umayado]] ([[Shotoku Taishi]]) reportedly began construction of [[Hōkōji]] (later {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥寺}}).
Today, [[Shinto shrines]] in [[Chichibu region|Chichibu]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], continue to distribute [[ofuda]] featuring wolves, sustaining the faith<ref>[http://www9.plala.or.jp/sinsi/07sinsi/fukuda/ohkami/ohkami-07.html The wolf depicted on shrine ofuda]</ref>. Further south, atop [[Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|Mount Mitake]] in [[Tokyo]], {{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} preserves a legend, based on 『{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}』, about "Oinu-sama" ("Sacred Dog"). During the [[Eastern Expedition of Yamato Takeru|eastern expedition of Yamato Takeru]], an evil god appeared as a giant deer, which was defeated with [[wild garlic]] (nobiru). Subsequently, a white [[wolf]] emerged from nowhere to guide Yamato Takeru and his army safely, leading him to proclaim the wolf as "Ōkuchi no Makami," who would remain there as a protector. From the [[Edo period]]'s [[Tenpō]] era onward, Ōkuchi no Makami became revered for its power to repel theft and malevolent spirits. The shrine’s bronze komainu (guardian dogs) date back to 1807, while the bronze statue in front of the main hall was crafted by [[Seibo Kitamura]] in 1985<ref>[http://musashimitakejinja.jp/yuisho/oinusama/ The reverence for wolves as sacred beings at Musashi Mitake Shrine]</ref>.
